title: Severe Acute Gallstone Pancreatitis in 52-Year-Old Man with Ranson Score 4 + Atlanta Severe Classification: Aggressive Lactated Ringer's Fluid Resuscitation, Opioid Pain Management (No NSAIDs), Early Enteral Nutrition via NJ Tube, Emergent ERCP with Sphincterotomy for Biliary Obstruction, Interval Cholecystectomy, and Multi-System Complication Surveillance
subtitle: ACUTE PANCREATITIS = inflammation of pancreas with diverse etiologies (top causes: GALLSTONES 40% + ALCOHOL 30% + idiopathic + post-ERCP + hypertriglyceridemia >1000 + hypercalcemia + drugs + autoimmune + trauma + infections + neoplasms); DIAGNOSIS requires 2 of 3 ATLANTA 2012 CRITERIA: (1) ABDOMINAL PAIN consistent with pancreatitis (epigastric + radiating to back + worse supine + better leaning forward + N/V + abdominal distension); (2) LIPASE OR AMYLASE >3× upper limit of normal (lipase more specific + sensitive than amylase + remains elevated longer); (3) IMAGING findings of acute pancreatitis (CT abdomen with IV contrast typically 72 hr after onset to assess severity + complications; US for gallstone evaluation). SEVERITY per Atlanta 2012 = MILD (no organ failure + no local + systemic complications); MODERATELY SEVERE (transient organ failure <48 hr or local/systemic complications); SEVERE (PERSISTENT organ failure >48 hr in 1 or more systems — cardiovascular SBP <90 + respiratory PaO2/FiO2 <300 + renal Cr >1.9 — using modified Marshall scoring); RANSON CRITERIA (severity prediction at admission + 48 hr): AT ADMISSION = age >55 + WBC >16,000 + glucose >200 + AST >250 + LDH >350 (gallstone slightly different); AT 48 HR = HCT drop >10 + BUN rise >5 + calcium <8 + PaO2 <60 + base deficit >4 + fluid sequestration >6L; SCORE 0-2 = mild (<1% mortality); 3-4 = MODERATE (15% mortality — this patient); 5-6 = severe (40% mortality); 7+ = very severe (~100% mortality). APACHE II + BISAP also commonly used. AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION = LACTATED RINGER'S (LR) preferred over NS (LR less hyperchloremic acidosis + may reduce SIRS); 5-10 mL/kg/hr (250-500 mL/hr in 70 kg adult) × 24-48 hr OR until hemodynamic improvement + UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr + BUN downtrending; AVOID excessive fluid (associated with worse outcomes — ARDS + abdominal compartment syndrome + intra-abdominal HTN); MONITOR for fluid overload + serial assessment + reassess q2-4h; bolus only for shock. PAIN MANAGEMENT = OPIOIDS first-line (morphine + hydromorphone + fentanyl) — historical concern about morphine causing sphincter of Oddi spasm worsening pancreatitis NOT supported by current evidence; MEPERIDINE avoided historically + now also acceptable but has CNS toxic metabolite normeperidine especially in renal impairment + elderly; AVOID NSAIDs in pancreatitis (renal injury risk + GI bleeding + may worsen pancreatic inflammation); acetaminophen + IV opioids + PCA + multimodal; PCA pump common. EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION = preferred over parenteral (TPN) per current evidence — reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity; NPO traditionally but EARLY (within 24-72 hr) ENTERAL feeding via nasojejunal (NJ) tube past ligament of Treitz to bypass pancreatic stimulation OR NG feeding (recent evidence comparable per studies) with low residue + lower fat + standard formulas; TPN only if enteral not tolerated; ORAL diet advance when pain controlled + tolerating + decreasing inflammatory markers. ANTIBIOTICS NOT ROUTINELY indicated for acute pancreatitis (does not improve outcomes + selects for resistance); RESERVED FOR infected necrosis (clinical deterioration + organ failure + persistent fever + gas in pancreas on CT + positive cultures) or extra-pancreatic infections (cholangitis + pneumonia); broad-spectrum + ID consultation. ERCP for GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS with cholangitis or persistent obstruction = within 24-72 hr; URGENT (24 hr) if cholangitis; sphincterotomy + stone extraction; reduces complications + may shorten course. CHOLECYSTECTOMY for gallstone pancreatitis = same-admission (preferably) for mild cases; interval cholecystectomy after recovery for severe cases (typically 4-6 weeks); reduces recurrence. COMPLICATIONS surveillance = LOCAL (peripancreatic fluid collections + pseudocyst + walled-off necrosis + infected necrosis + hemorrhage + portal vein thrombosis + ascites) + SYSTEMIC (ARDS + AKI + cardiovascular shock + sepsis + DIC + hyperglycemia + hypocalcemia from saponification + ileus + abdominal compartment syndrome). MULTIDISCIPLINARY = gastroenterology + general surgery + critical care + pain medicine + nutrition + interventional radiology if indicated

labs.v: 52 y/o male F. Castro presents to ED at 0830 today with 1 day of progressive severe epigastric pain radiating to back + N/V × 8 episodes + inability to keep down fluids + worsening since dinner last night (large fatty meal + 3 beers — both gallstone + alcohol pancreatitis triggers). PMH: cholelithiasis × 5 years (gallstones on US 5 yr ago when investigated for episodic biliary colic; declined elective cholecystectomy at that time due to mild symptoms); HTN (lisinopril 10 mg daily — controlled); type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg BID — HbA1c 7.8); hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 40 mg daily); obesity (BMI 32); episodic biliary colic with meals × several years intermittent; remote moderate alcohol use (2-3 beers most weekends; not chronic heavy use; recent fatty meal + beer last night may have been trigger). SOCIAL: married 23 yr to wife (50, accountant); 3 adult children (2 sons + daughter; all nearby); works as engineer; insurance excellent; lives in suburban home; supportive family; cultural background Mexican-American + bilingual home + family identifies as Catholic. PRESENTING ILLNESS: dinner last night = large fatty Mexican meal + 3 beers + onset of postprandial RUQ + epigastric pain (similar to biliary colic episodes in past); pain worsened overnight + radiating to back + becoming SEVERE 9/10 + non-relieved by position + nausea + repeated vomiting + dehydration + pallor + diaphoresis; arrived ED 0830. CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: appearing acutely uncomfortable + lying still + minimizing movement + leaning slightly forward + severe epigastric pain radiating to back; mild jaundice + scleral icterus (biliary obstruction); diaphoresis + tachycardia + tachypnea + dehydration; abdomen distended + tender + guarding voluntary + decreased bowel sounds + no rebound + no peritoneal signs (no perforation); no Cullen's sign (periumbilical bruising — late finding); no Grey-Turner's sign (flank bruising — late finding); GCS 15 + alert + oriented; weight 96 kg (BMI 32 obese). LABS revealed acute pancreatitis with biliary obstruction: LIPASE 1200 (>3× ULN — diagnostic for pancreatitis per Atlanta 2012); AMYLASE 850 (elevated; less specific); AST 285 + ALT 220 (>3× normal — suggests biliary obstruction in gallstone pancreatitis); total bilirubin 4.8 + direct 3.2 + alkaline phosphatase 320 + GGT 280 (cholestatic pattern — biliary obstruction); lactate 3.8 (elevated — perfusion); WBC 18,500 (leukocytosis SIRS); CRP 220 (inflammatory); BUN 28 + Cr 1.3 (mild AKI from dehydration); glucose 195 (mild hyperglycemia); calcium 7.6 (LOW — saponification with fatty acids common in severe pancreatitis); magnesium 1.4 + phosphate 2.4 (low); triglycerides 180 (normal — rules out hyperTG pancreatitis); coags normal + fibrinogen 480 (acute phase). CT ABDOMEN with IV contrast at 1030: acute pancreatitis with mild peripancreatic edema + no necrosis identified at this early time point (CT severity better at 72 hr after onset); GALLSTONES in gallbladder; common bile duct (CBD) dilated to 12 mm (normal <8 mm) + IMPACTED STONE at ampulla of Vater = GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS with biliary obstruction. ATLANTA 2012 CRITERIA met: pain + lipase >3× ULN + imaging findings = diagnosis confirmed. SEVERITY ASSESSMENT: RANSON CRITERIA at admission = 4 (WBC >16,000 + AST >250 + LDH >350 pending; predicts moderate-severe ~15% mortality); ATLANTA classification pending 48 hr — patient already has organ dysfunction (mild AKI Cr 1.3 + early ARDS pattern PaO2 76 on 2L NC + lactate 3.8) → progressing to SEVERE pancreatitis (organ failure persisting >48 hr); APACHE II + BISAP also commonly used. MICU ADMISSION activated for severe acute pancreatitis with aggressive resuscitation + multidisciplinary engagement.
labs.r: ACUTE PANCREATITIS diagnosis requires 2 of 3 Atlanta criteria (pain + lipase/amylase >3× ULN + imaging); SEVERITY assessment via Ranson + Atlanta + APACHE II + BISAP. This patient = severe acute gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction + early organ dysfunction requiring MICU + aggressive resuscitation + ERCP + supportive care + complications surveillance.

labs.v: AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION = cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management; goal = restore tissue perfusion + prevent organ failure + complications; LACTATED RINGER'S (LR) preferred over normal saline (NS) — multiple RCTs show LR associated with less hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis + may reduce SIRS + improve outcomes; INITIAL DOSING = 5-10 mL/kg/hr × 24-48 hr based on hemodynamic + clinical response (96 kg × 5 = 480 mL/hr; can be 480-960 mL/hr initially); REASSESS Q2-4H — target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr (in 96 kg = >48 mL/hr); BUN downtrending (initial 28 → goal <20); HCT downtrending (from concentrated state at admission); hemodynamic improvement (HR + BP); lactate normalization; resolution of acidosis. AVOID EXCESSIVE FLUID OVERLOAD (associated with worse outcomes — ARDS + abdominal compartment syndrome + intra-abdominal hypertension + cardiovascular complications) — recent evidence (WATERFALL trial 2022) suggests MODERATE aggressive fluid (1.5 mL/kg/hr × 1 hr then 1.5 mL/kg/hr maintenance) may be as effective as more aggressive (250-500 mL/hr) with less fluid overload; INDIVIDUALIZED based on patient + severity + response. THIS PATIENT received LR at 500 mL/hr (5.2 mL/kg/hr) initial × 24 hr then 250 mL/hr × 12 hr then 150 mL/hr maintenance based on response. MONITORING: continuous cardiac monitoring + arterial line for BP + central venous pressure (if utility) + Foley for hourly UOP + I/O hourly + daily weights + serial labs (BMP + LFTs + lipase + CRP + lactate q4-12h initially → q12h → daily); abdominal exam + bowel sounds + signs of ileus or abdominal compartment syndrome (abdomen distended + tense + decreased UOP + organ failure); pulmonary assessment + risk of ARDS; CARDIAC + RESPIRATORY + RENAL + HEPATIC + NEUROLOGIC organ surveillance. ELECTROLYTE replacement: hypocalcemia common (saponification — replace calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly with cardiac monitoring); hypomagnesemia (replace IV); hypophosphatemia (replace; common in refeeding); hypokalemia + sodium balance per BMP. GLUCOSE management — hyperglycemia common in pancreatitis (insulin sliding scale + target 140-180 in ICU; AVOID hypoglycemia in critically ill); insulin gtt if needed. NUTRITION (covered separately) — early enteral preferred over TPN.
labs.r: AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION with Lactated Ringer's is cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management — restores perfusion + prevents organ failure + reduces complications. Monitor closely + avoid excessive overload (ARDS + compartment syndrome). Reassess q2-4h based on UOP + BUN + clinical response. Replace electrolytes (calcium + magnesium + phosphate). RN role: fluid administration + monitoring + electrolyte replacement + escalation.

labs.n: PAIN MANAGEMENT — opioids first-line + avoid NSAIDs
labs.v: PAIN MANAGEMENT in acute pancreatitis: PAIN is severe + often refractory + requires aggressive management; OPIOIDS first-line — MORPHINE + HYDROMORPHONE + FENTANYL acceptable; HISTORICAL concern about MORPHINE causing SPHINCTER OF ODDI SPASM worsening pancreatitis NOT supported by current evidence (most studies show no clinically significant difference + opioids do not worsen pancreatitis); MEPERIDINE (Demerol) was historically preferred for this reason but is no longer first-line + has TOXIC METABOLITE NORMEPERIDINE accumulating with renal impairment + elderly + chronic use → seizures + neurotoxicity; ROUTES = IV preferred (rapid onset + titratable); PCA pump common for patient-controlled administration; ORAL transition as tolerated. DOSING: HYDROMORPHONE 0.5-1 mg IV q3-4h PRN + PCA 0.2 mg/dose with 10 min lock-out + max 1.2 mg/hr; MORPHINE 4-10 mg IV q4h PRN + PCA equivalent; FENTANYL 25-50 mcg IV q1h PRN (less histamine + better in renal impairment); SCHEDULED ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg IV/PO q6h (opioid-sparing + antipyretic + non-NSAID); ANTI-EMETIC ondansetron 4-8 mg IV q6-8h PRN + alternative scopolamine + prochlorperazine + dimenhydrinate. AVOID NSAIDs (ibuprofen + naproxen + ketorolac + indomethacin) in acute pancreatitis: (1) RENAL injury risk in volume-depleted patient + AKI common in pancreatitis; (2) GI BLEEDING + ulcers (already inflamed GI tract); (3) MAY WORSEN pancreatic inflammation in some studies + theoretical inhibition of beneficial inflammatory response; (4) bleeding risk in patient who may need ERCP + cholecystectomy; (5) interfere with assessment (anti-inflammatory + antipyretic masks signs). NON-PHARMACOLOGIC pain management: positioning (knees to chest or sitting forward — relieves pressure on pancreas), warm compress to abdomen, distraction + relaxation + breathing techniques, music + family presence + chaplain spiritual support, minimize movement, NPO initially (avoid stimulation), nasogastric decompression if vomiting persistent (not routine but may help). MULTIMODAL pain management = opioid PCA + scheduled acetaminophen + anti-emetic + non-pharmacologic + transition to oral opioids (oxycodone 5-10 mg PO q4-6h PRN) as tolerating. CHRONIC PAIN issues in pancreatitis: chronic pancreatitis can develop after recurrent acute episodes + may require chronic pain management; avoid escalation + risk of opioid use disorder; multimodal approach + pancreatic enzyme replacement + alcohol/triglyceride/gallstone management + interventional procedures (celiac plexus block + pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy PERT) + chronic pain specialist + outpatient management.
labs.r: PAIN MANAGEMENT in acute pancreatitis = OPIOIDS first-line (hydromorphone + morphine + fentanyl; meperidine no longer preferred + has toxic metabolite); PCA pump + scheduled acetaminophen + anti-emetic + multimodal; AVOID NSAIDs (renal + GI + may worsen pancreatic inflammation + bleeding risk for ERCP/surgery). RN role: pain assessment + medication administration + monitoring + escalation + non-pharmacologic + family education.

labs.n: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION + NPO + nutrition support
labs.v: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION in acute pancreatitis = paradigm shift over past 15 years; HISTORICAL approach was NPO + TPN (parenteral) for prolonged periods to 'rest the pancreas'; CURRENT EVIDENCE strongly supports EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION (within 24-72 hours of admission for mild-moderate cases; within 72 hours for severe cases when stable) — reduces infections (especially bacterial translocation from gut), shortens LOS, reduces mortality, supports gut integrity, reduces TPN-associated complications (bacterial translocation + line infections + cholestasis + hyperglycemia). NPO initially typically for first 24-48 hr while resuscitating + assessing severity + managing pain + nausea; ORAL diet advance when tolerating (clear liquids → soft diet → regular as pain controlled + tolerating). FOR PATIENTS NOT TOLERATING ORAL (severe + N/V persistent + pain refractory + concerns about gastric stimulation) = NASOENTERAL FEEDING preferred via: (1) NASOJEJUNAL (NJ) TUBE past ligament of Treitz — historically thought to bypass pancreatic stimulation + reduce pancreatic enzyme release; placed by GI/IR or endoscopy; (2) NASOGASTRIC (NG) feeding — recent meta-analyses (multiple RCTs) show COMPARABLE EFFICACY to NJ feeding; easier placement + may be preferred; (3) FORMULATIONS = semi-elemental (low-residue + lower fat + pre-digested protein + medium-chain triglycerides) preferred for pancreatic patients; standard polymeric formulas acceptable as well; (4) ADVANCEMENT = start 10-25 mL/hr + advance per protocol + goal 1-1.5 kcal/mL of formula at appropriate volume to meet caloric needs; (5) protein 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day in severe + ICU patients; (6) avoid bolus feeding (continuous + may need cyclic). TPN ROLE = reserved for patients NOT TOLERATING ENTERAL nutrition (refractory ileus + obstruction + severe abdominal compartment syndrome) — typically after 5-7 days of failed enteral; multiple complications (line infections + bacterial translocation + hyperglycemia + cholestasis + LFTs). REFEEDING SYNDROME considerations — chronic alcohol use + malnutrition + chronic pancreatitis can have refeeding syndrome with rapid nutrition (hypophosphatemia + hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia + thiamine deficiency); START LOW + GO SLOW + monitor electrolytes + replace BEFORE feeding + thiamine BEFORE carbs. THIS PATIENT plan: NPO Day 1 with aggressive fluid resuscitation + pain management; NJ tube placement at end of Day 2 ERCP + semi-elemental formula at 25 mL/hr starting Day 3 + advancing per protocol; transition to oral diet (clear liquids → soft → regular) as tolerating + pain controlled + LFTs improving; nutrition consult ongoing; pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) not needed for acute uncomplicated case but considered if chronic pancreatitis develops.
labs.r: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION (within 24-72 hr) preferred over TPN in acute pancreatitis per current evidence — reduces infections + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity; NJ vs NG tube comparable per recent evidence; semi-elemental formula; advance per protocol; AVOID prolonged NPO + TPN unless enteral truly not tolerated. RN role: NJ tube placement assistance + formula administration + monitoring + advancement + electrolyte monitoring + family education.

labs.v: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography) for GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS with cholangitis or biliary obstruction: TIMING = URGENT (within 24 hr) if CHOLANGITIS (fever + jaundice + RUQ pain = Charcot's triad; or shock + altered mental status added = Reynolds' pentad — emergent + life-threatening); ELECTIVE-URGENT (within 72 hr) for persistent biliary obstruction without cholangitis; relative urgency for documented obstructing stones with worsening pancreatitis. PROCEDURE = endoscopic sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stenting if residual edema + stricture; clearing common bile duct (CBD); diagnostic + therapeutic; usually performed by GI or HPB surgeon with interventional capability. RISKS of ERCP: (1) POST-ERCP PANCREATITIS (5-10% incidence) — most common complication; risk factors = young + female + history of pancreatitis + difficult cannulation + sphincterotomy of pancreatic duct + manipulation; prevention with PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after procedure (NSAID protective effect on post-ERCP pancreatitis); aggressive hydration; pancreatic duct stent for high-risk patients; recognize + manage if occurs; (2) BLEEDING from sphincterotomy site; (3) PERFORATION (rare; 0.5-1%); (4) infection (cholangitis + cholecystitis worsening); (5) anesthesia risks (procedural sedation + airway). PRE-ERCP — antibiotics if suspected cholangitis (ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam); coagulation status normal; consent + family + multidisciplinary; INR <1.5 + platelets >50k; address fluid + electrolytes + pain. POST-ERCP — monitoring for complications + serial lipase + recurrent pain + cardiopulmonary monitoring × 24-48 hr in high-risk patients; continue antibiotics if cholangitis; advance diet as tolerating + pain controlled. CHOLECYSTECTOMY for gallstone pancreatitis: (1) MILD CASES = same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy (preferably during initial hospitalization after pancreatitis improving + tolerating PO + within 1-2 weeks); reduces recurrence + readmissions; some institutions do within 48-72 hr if mild; (2) SEVERE CASES = INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY after recovery (typically 4-6 weeks; allow complete resolution of pancreatitis + complications); maintain ERCP + sphincterotomy in interim for biliary protection; (3) CHRONIC GALLBLADDER ISSUES post-cholecystectomy = post-cholecystectomy syndrome rare but can occur; (4) ALTERNATIVE for high-surgical-risk patients = ERCP + sphincterotomy alone (without cholecystectomy) for biliary access + prevention; (5) CHOLECYSTECTOMY recurrence prevention essential — without it, recurrent gallstone pancreatitis rate ~20-50% within months. THIS PATIENT plan: ERCP within 24-72 hr (Day 2) with sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stent if needed; INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY at 4-6 weeks after recovery from severe pancreatitis (vs same-admission for mild cases); general surgery aware + outpatient follow-up + scheduling.
labs.r: ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis (within 24 hr) or biliary obstruction (within 24-72 hr) — sphincterotomy + stone extraction + stent; complications = post-ERCP pancreatitis (5-10%; prevent with rectal indomethacin) + bleeding + perforation + infection. CHOLECYSTECTOMY = same-admission for mild + interval (4-6 wk) for severe to prevent recurrence. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + monitoring + family education + scheduling.

labs.v: ACUTE PANCREATITIS COMPLICATIONS surveillance + management: (1) LOCAL — PERIPANCREATIC FLUID COLLECTIONS (occur within first 4 weeks; usually resorb spontaneously); PSEUDOCYST (fluid collection persisting >4 weeks; encapsulated; may require drainage if symptomatic + endoscopic ultrasound-guided drainage); WALLED-OFF NECROSIS (later; necrotic material + fluid in encapsulated cavity); INFECTED NECROSIS (clinical deterioration + organ failure + persistent fever + gas in pancreas on CT + positive cultures; antibiotic therapy + percutaneous drainage + endoscopic step-up approach + minimally invasive necrosectomy; surgery if refractory); HEMORRHAGE from pancreatic vasculature erosion (IR embolization + surgery); PORTAL VEIN THROMBOSIS + SPLENIC VEIN THROMBOSIS (anticoagulation if no contraindication); ASCITES + pleural effusions; (2) SYSTEMIC — ARDS (acute respiratory distress syndrome — bilateral pulmonary edema noncardiogenic from inflammatory + fluid; supplemental O2 → HFNC → NIPPV → mechanical ventilation; lung-protective ventilation Vt 6 mL/kg IBW + PEEP); ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (Cr rising + UOP dropping; from hypovolemia + cytokine + abdominal compartment; aggressive fluid initial then careful + CRRT if severe); CARDIOVASCULAR SHOCK (distributive + hypovolemic + cardiogenic mixed; norepinephrine first-line + supportive); SEPSIS from infected necrosis + bacterial translocation + cholangitis (low threshold to treat broad-spectrum antibiotics + ID consult); DIC (rare); HYPERGLYCEMIA (inflammation + stress + DM + insulin sliding scale + close monitoring); HYPOCALCEMIA (saponification — fatty acid soap formation with calcium — replace empirically + monitor + cardiac monitoring; severe hypoCa can cause arrhythmia + tetany + Chvostek + Trousseau); HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA + HYPOMAGNESEMIA + HYPOKALEMIA; ILEUS + DELAYED GASTRIC EMPTYING + abdominal distension; INTRA-ABDOMINAL HYPERTENSION + ABDOMINAL COMPARTMENT SYNDROME (intra-abdominal pressure >20 mmHg + organ failure — decompressive laparotomy if severe + refractory); thromboembolism + DVT/PE (prophylaxis with mechanical SCDs + heparin SC); pneumonia + line infections + UTI nosocomial; encephalopathy (uremia + hepatic if cholestasis severe + inflammation); pruritus from cholestasis; SECONDARY ORGAN FAILURE — multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS); chronic complications = chronic pancreatitis + diabetes + exocrine insufficiency + chronic pain + recurrent episodes + nutritional deficiencies. MULTIDISCIPLINARY for complications = MICU + GI + surgery + IR + pulmonology + nephrology + ID + nutrition + pain medicine + palliative care if severe + multidisciplinary rounds. PROGNOSIS = mortality 15% in severe per Ranson + Atlanta + APACHE II; higher if infected necrosis + multi-organ failure + persistent organ failure; LONG-TERM = full recovery typical for mild + moderate; chronic complications possible for severe + recurrent.
labs.r: ACUTE PANCREATITIS COMPLICATIONS = local (fluid collections + pseudocyst + walled-off necrosis + infected necrosis + hemorrhage + thrombosis + ascites) + systemic (ARDS + AKI + shock + sepsis + DIC + electrolyte + abdominal compartment + organ failure). Multidisciplinary surveillance + management. RN role: monitoring + escalation + multidisciplinary + family education.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: NO acute respiratory failure currently (oxygenating on 2L NC + improving; will monitor for ARDS); NO need for vasopressors currently (hemodynamic improving with fluid resuscitation alone); NO acute MI or arrhythmia (cardiac monitor normal + ECG no acute changes); NO acute stroke; NO peritoneal signs or perforation; NO acute encephalopathy beyond mild from pain + medications; NO active bleeding (coags normal + Hgb stable); NO acute hypocalcemia tetany (calcium 7.6 mild low + corrected + cardiac monitor); NO acute pancreatic necrosis on initial CT (will reassess at 72 hr); NO active cholangitis (Charcot's triad not met currently — has jaundice + RUQ pain but no fever pattern + cultures pending); NO acute infected necrosis (early in course; will surveillance); NO acute DIC (coags + platelets stable); NO refractory severe hypocalcemia (replacing with calcium gluconate); NO acute mental health crisis; family supportive + appropriate; insurance + multidisciplinary team available + experienced. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: PROMPT recognition by ED + appropriate diagnosis + classification + risk stratification (Ranson + Atlanta); AGGRESSIVE early fluid resuscitation with Lactated Ringer's + close monitoring; MULTIDISCIPLINARY team engaged (MICU + GI + general surgery + ID + nutrition + pain medicine); ICU LEVEL care with arterial line + central line + continuous monitoring + frequent reassessment; ERCP planned within 24-72 hr (appropriate timing for gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction); EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION planned post-ERCP via NJ tube (per current evidence); APPROPRIATE pain management with opioid PCA + scheduled acetaminophen + AVOIDING NSAIDs; ELECTROLYTE replacement + hydration + nutrition support; FAMILY supportive + extended family available + insurance excellent + multidisciplinary outpatient care accessible + interval cholecystectomy planned for recurrence prevention; PATIENT alert + cooperative + receptive + ENGAGED with care + family translation supported (bilingual home); CULTURAL + SPIRITUAL support (Catholic + chaplain available); modifiable risk factors addressable (diet + alcohol + obesity + diabetes management); chronic disease management opportunity. STANDARD expected positive trajectory with comprehensive intervention.
labs.r: Despite serious initial presentation + severe acute pancreatitis with biliary obstruction + organ dysfunction, multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS support good outcome: prompt recognition + aggressive resuscitation + multidisciplinary team + ICU care + ERCP + early enteral nutrition + family + insurance + cultural sensitivity. Standard expected positive trajectory with comprehensive intervention + interval cholecystectomy preventing recurrence.

nursesNote: 1030 — 52 y/o male F. Castro admitted to MICU Bed 4 via ED for severe acute gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction. HISTORY: 1 day of progressive severe epigastric pain radiating to back + N/V × 8 episodes + worsening since dinner last night (large fatty Mexican meal + 3 beers). PMH: cholelithiasis × 5 yr (declined elective cholecystectomy at that time + intermittent biliary colic); HTN (lisinopril controlled); T2DM (metformin 1000 BID + HbA1c 7.8); hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 40 mg); obesity (BMI 32); moderate alcohol (2-3 beers most weekends); episodic biliary colic. SOCIAL: married 23 yr to wife + 3 adult children + Mexican-American + bilingual + Catholic family + insurance excellent. ED ASSESSMENT 0830: HR 118 sinus tach + BP 104/68 MAP 80 + RR 26 + SpO2 94% RA + T 38.4°C + weight 96 kg + severe epigastric pain 9/10 radiating to back + lying still + leaning forward + mild jaundice + scleral icterus + diaphoresis + abdomen distended + tender + guarding voluntary + decreased bowel sounds + no peritoneal signs. INTERVENTIONS in ED: 2 large-bore peripheral IVs (16-gauge) + Foley + cardiac monitor + pulse ox + IV LACTATED RINGER'S (NOT NS — less hyperchloremic acidosis + may reduce SIRS) at 500 mL/hr initial (5.2 mL/kg/hr); LABS sent — CBC + BMP + LFT + LIPASE 1200 + AMYLASE 850 + AST 285 + ALT 220 + T bili 4.8 (direct 3.2) + alk phos 320 + GGT 280 (CHOLESTATIC pattern + biliary obstruction; AST:ALT >1 with mod elevation suggests biliary obstruction in gallstone pancreatitis); lactate 3.8 (elevated) + WBC 18,500 (leukocytosis SIRS) + CRP 220 (markedly elevated) + procalcitonin 0.6 (mild) + Hgb 14.2 + platelets 220 + Na 138 + K 4.1 + Cl 102 + HCO3 18 (mild metabolic acidosis) + BUN 28 + Cr 1.3 (mild AKI from dehydration; baseline 0.9) + glucose 195 + CALCIUM 7.6 (LOW — saponification with fatty acids common in severe pancreatitis) + Mg 1.4 + Phos 2.4 (low) + triglycerides 180 (normal — rules out hyperTG) + coags normal + fibrinogen 480 (acute phase); ABG pH 7.32 + PCO2 28 + PaO2 76 on 2L NC (mild metabolic acidosis + early respiratory dysfunction). CT ABDOMEN with IV contrast at 1030 = ACUTE PANCREATITIS with mild peripancreatic edema + no necrosis identified at this early time point (CT severity better at 72 hr after onset) + GALLSTONES in gallbladder + CBD DILATED to 12 mm + IMPACTED STONE at AMPULLA OF VATER = GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS with BILIARY OBSTRUCTION. SEVERITY ASSESSMENT: ATLANTA 2012 CRITERIA met (pain + lipase >3× ULN + imaging findings); RANSON CRITERIA at admission = 4 (WBC >16,000 + AST >250 + LDH >350; predicts moderate-severe ~15% mortality); ATLANTA classification pending 48 hr — patient already has organ dysfunction (mild AKI Cr 1.3 + early ARDS pattern PaO2 76 on 2L NC + lactate 3.8) → progressing to SEVERE pancreatitis. ED treatment: HYDROMORPHONE 1 mg IV bolus + PCA pump set 0.2 mg/dose lock-out 10 min max 1.2 mg/hr (OPIOID PAIN management first-line in pancreatitis; AVOID NSAIDs — renal injury + GI bleed + bleeding risk for upcoming ERCP); SCHEDULED ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg IV q6h (opioid-sparing + antipyretic + non-NSAID); ONDANSETRON 4 mg IV q6h PRN nausea; pain 9 → 5/10. MICU ADMISSION 1200: HR 98 NSR + BP 122/76 MAP 91 (stable on LR) + RR 20 + SpO2 97% on 2L NC + T 37.6°C (decreased with acetaminophen) + pain 5/10 + GCS 15 + arterial line + central line placed by ICU intensivist + IV LR continued at 250 mL/hr (reduced from initial 500 once hemodynamic improved); UOP 30 mL/hr × 1 hr (initially decreased; goal >0.5 mL/kg/hr = >48 mL/hr — needs to improve); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slowly (over 10 min with cardiac monitoring for hypocalcemia; common in severe pancreatitis from saponification); MAGNESIUM 2 g IV (low); phosphate replacement; insulin sliding scale for hyperglycemia (target 140-180 in ICU; AVOID hypoglycemia in critically ill); MULTIDISCIPLINARY engagement — GI + GENERAL SURGERY + INFECTIOUS DISEASE + NUTRITION + PAIN MEDICINE consultations; ERCP scheduled for tomorrow 0900 (within 24-72 hr for gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction); NUTRITION consult for early enteral nutrition planning via NJ tube past ligament of Treitz post-ERCP. MICU 2000 (8 hr): HR 92 + BP 128/78 + RR 18 + SpO2 97% on 2L NC + T 37.4 + pain 4/10 on PCA + nausea resolved + UOP 60 mL/hr (improved with continued fluid); total IVF past 12 hr ~6.5 L; BUN 24 (downtrending — fluid responsive); Cr 1.1 (improving); calcium 8.4 after replacement + albumin-corrected 9.0; Mg 1.9; ERCP scheduled tomorrow + NJ tube placement at end of ERCP. FAMILY (wife + 3 adult children) at bedside throughout; transparent + compassionate communication + chaplain coordinated (Catholic family) + Spanish interpreter available + family supportive + asking informed questions. DAY 2 morning 0700 (pre-ERCP): HR 84 + BP 128/76 + RR 16 + SpO2 98% on RA (weaning from 2L) + T 37.2 + alert + comfortable + pain 3/10 + tolerating sips of clear water with ice chips; UOP 80 mL/hr; total IVF past 24 hr ~10 L; RANSON 48 HR CRITERIA: HCT drop NO + BUN rise NO (down) + calcium <8 YES (initial 7.6) + PaO2 <60 NO + base deficit >4 borderline + fluid sequestration >6L YES at 10 L; CUMULATIVE RANSON 4 (mild-moderate; 15% mortality predicted); LABS: LIPASE 850 (downtrending from 1200) + AST 220 + ALT 180 + BILIRUBIN 6.2 (peaked) + WBC 16,500 (downtrending) + CRP 180 + lactate 2.0 + calcium 8.4 + Mg 2.0 + Phos 2.4 + albumin 3.4. DAY 2 0900-1100 ERCP: performed by GI + interventional team — sphincterotomy + stone extraction (1.5 cm stone removed from ampulla of Vater + CBD cleared) + biliary stent placed for any residual edema; procedure tolerated well + no immediate complications; PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg given immediately after procedure (NSAID protective effect on post-ERCP pancreatitis); awake + alert post-procedure + back to MICU; ERCP-related post-procedure pancreatitis monitoring × 48 hr (5-10% incidence). NJ TUBE placement at end of ERCP successful (past ligament of Treitz); early enteral nutrition planning. DAY 3 morning (post-ERCP + early enteral nutrition): HR 82 + BP 130/78 + RR 16 + SpO2 98% RA + T 37.0 + alert + pain 2/10 + transitioning from PCA hydromorphone to PO oxycodone 5 mg q4-6h PRN + ERCP-related no new symptoms + EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION started via NJ tube — semi-elemental formula (low-residue + low-fat + bypass pancreatic stimulation) at 25 mL/hr advancing per protocol; bilirubin downtrending 4.6 + lipase 420 + AST 140 + ALT 100; WBC 14,000 + CRP 120; calcium 8.8; UOP 100 mL/hr; total IVF past 48 hr ~14 L; transitioning to maintenance fluids + monitoring. INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY planning — at 4-6 weeks after recovery from severe pancreatitis (vs same-admission for mild cases) per general surgery; outpatient follow-up + scheduling. Patient + family appropriate + receptive + engaged; chaplain ongoing; nutrition + GI + general surgery + ID + pain medicine continuing engagement. PLAN: continue MICU monitoring + complications surveillance + advance enteral nutrition + transition to oral diet (clear liquids → soft → regular) when tolerating + pain controlled + LFTs improving + lipase downtrending + advance to floor when stable + INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY 4-6 weeks; outpatient GI + general surgery + PCP medical home + diabetes + lifestyle + family supportive + community + multidisciplinary lifelong management.
providerOrders: ADMIT to Medicine ICU Bed 4 (single-room continuous monitoring; family-centered environment)

DIAGNOSIS: severe acute gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction (impacted stone at ampulla of Vater + CBD dilated to 12 mm); Ranson 4 (moderate-severe predicted ~15% mortality); Atlanta classification pending 48 hr — early organ dysfunction; T2DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity
CODE STATUS: FULL CODE; healthcare proxy = wife; decisional capacity present
AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION:
LACTATED RINGER'S (LR — NOT normal saline; LR less hyperchloremic acidosis + may reduce SIRS)
INITIAL 500 mL/hr (5-10 mL/kg/hr × 24-48 hr) until hemodynamic improvement + UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr
REASSESS Q2-4H — target UOP >48 mL/hr (>0.5 mL/kg/hr) + BUN downtrending + hemodynamic stability + lactate clearance
AVOID excessive overload (ARDS + abdominal compartment syndrome risk)
Transition to maintenance fluids (150 mL/hr) when responsive + tolerating + cumulative fluid balance positive
Continuous monitoring + arterial line + central line + Foley + I/O hourly + daily weights
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
HYDROMORPHONE PCA 0.2 mg/dose lock-out 10 min + max 1.2 mg/hr (opioid first-line; AVOID meperidine — toxic metabolite + AVOID NSAIDs — renal + GI bleed + bleeding risk for ERCP)
ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg IV q6h scheduled (opioid-sparing + antipyretic + non-NSAID)
Transition to PO oxycodone 5-10 mg q4-6h PRN when tolerating + improving
Non-pharmacologic — positioning + warm compress + distraction + relaxation + family + chaplain
ONDANSETRON 4-8 mg IV q6-8h PRN nausea + alternative scopolamine + prochlorperazine + dimenhydrinate
AVOID NSAIDs in acute pancreatitis (renal injury + GI bleeding + bleeding risk for ERCP/surgery + may worsen pancreatic inflammation)
ELECTROLYTE replacement + monitoring:
CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1-2 g IV slowly with cardiac monitoring (hypocalcemia common from saponification; replace q4-6h as needed; monitor + ECG + cardiac symptoms)
MAGNESIUM 2-4 g IV (low common in chronic alcohol/pancreatitis; replace + maintain Mg >2.0)
PHOSPHATE replacement if <2.0 (common in refeeding)
POTASSIUM replacement as needed; sodium balance per BMP
Q4-6H BMP + LFTs + lipase + calcium + magnesium + phosphate + lactate × first 24 hr then q12h then daily
GLUCOSE management:
Insulin sliding scale for hyperglycemia (target 140-180 in ICU; AVOID hypoglycemia)
Hourly fingerstick glucose initially → q2-4h
Insulin drip for glucose >250 not controlled by sliding scale
MONITORING (ICU-level):
Continuous cardiac monitoring + arterial line + central line + pulse oximetry
Q1H vital signs + UOP + cardiac/respiratory/neuro/abdominal assessment
Q4-6H labs trending × first 24 hr → q12h → daily
Daily CRP + procalcitonin + lipase trending

Complications surveillance — ARDS (CXR + ABG + SpO2); AKI (Cr + UOP); cardiovascular (BP + HR + lactate); hepatic (LFTs + bilirubin); hematologic (CBC + coags); abdominal compartment syndrome (intra-abdominal pressure via bladder pressure if concerns); ileus (bowel sounds + abdominal exam + NG output if NG placed); secondary infections (cultures + procalcitonin + clinical)
Pain assessment q4h + with each intervention
Family + chaplain + emotional support
ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis (URGENT within 24-72 hr; URGENT 24 hr if cholangitis):
Scheduled Day 2 0900 with GI + interventional team
Sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stent if residual edema
PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after procedure (NSAID protective for post-ERCP pancreatitis 5-10% incidence)
Pre-ERCP: NPO + coags (INR <1.5 + platelets >50k) + cultures if cholangitis suspected + antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole if cholangitis) + consent + family + multidisciplinary
Post-ERCP: continuous monitoring × 24-48 hr + serial lipase + recurrent pain assessment + advance diet as tolerating + pain controlled + continue antibiotics if cholangitis
NUTRITION management:
NPO initially Day 1 (resuscitation + assessment + severity)
EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION via NJ tube (past ligament of Treitz) starting Day 3 (within 24-72 hr per current evidence — reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity)
Semi-elemental formula (low-residue + low-fat + pre-digested protein + medium-chain triglycerides) at 25 mL/hr advancing per protocol
Goal 1-1.5 kcal/mL formula + protein 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day in severe + ICU
Continuous feeding (not bolus) + may need cyclic
Transition to oral diet (clear liquids → soft → regular) as tolerating + pain controlled + LFTs improving
NUTRITION consult + dietitian + multidisciplinary
Avoid TPN unless enteral truly not tolerated (refractory ileus + obstruction + severe abdominal compartment; reserved for 5-7 days of failed enteral)
ANTIBIOTICS:
NOT ROUTINELY indicated for acute pancreatitis (does not improve outcomes + selects for resistance)
Reserved for infected necrosis (clinical deterioration + organ failure + persistent fever + gas in pancreas on CT + positive cultures) or extra-pancreatic infections (cholangitis + pneumonia)
Pre-ERCP antibiotics if cholangitis suspected (ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin-tazobactam)
Broad-spectrum if infection suspected + ID consultation
MULTIDISCIPLINARY engagement:
Gastroenterology (Dr. Lee) + general surgery (Dr. Patel) + critical care + pain medicine + nutrition + interventional radiology if indicated + infectious disease
Multidisciplinary rounds daily + multidisciplinary care
Family-centered + chaplain + social worker + cultural sensitivity (Spanish interpreter available + Catholic family)
PCP medical home for chronic disease management + DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity + lifestyle
INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY:
Schedule INTERVAL laparoscopic cholecystectomy 4-6 weeks after recovery from severe pancreatitis (vs same-admission for mild cases) to prevent recurrence
General surgery aware + outpatient follow-up + scheduling
Pre-op evaluation + clearance + post-op recovery + supportive care
PROPHYLAXIS:

DVT prophylaxis — mechanical SCDs initially during fluid resuscitation + transition to subcutaneous heparin (5000 units SC q8h) or enoxaparin (40 mg SC daily) when bleeding risk acceptable + post-ERCP
Stress ulcer prophylaxis — pantoprazole 40 mg IV/PO daily
Skin protection + pressure injury prevention + Braden + repositioning
Mobility — early ambulation + bed-to-chair as tolerated + PT/OT bedside as needed
Oral chlorhexidine + oral care q4h + VAP bundle if intubated
Glycemic control
FAMILY-CENTERED + culturally sensitive care:
Family (wife + 3 adult children) at bedside throughout
Spanish interpreter available + bilingual home + Catholic family + chaplain coordinated
Transparent + compassionate communication + emotional support
Cultural + spiritual considerations (traditional foods + family rituals + religious practices)
Daily family meetings + multidisciplinary engagement + chaplain ongoing
Family education + nutrition + chronic disease management + recovery + lifelong management
DISCHARGE planning:
Outpatient GI follow-up within 2 weeks + general surgery for interval cholecystectomy 4-6 weeks
PCP medical home for DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity + lifestyle counseling
Nutrition counseling + Mediterranean-style diet + low-fat + weight management + diabetes management
Alcohol counseling + moderation or abstinence (alcohol-related pancreatitis trigger)
Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) consideration if exocrine insufficiency develops
Chronic pancreatitis screening at outpatient if recurrent episodes
Insurance + community resources
PATIENT + FAMILY EDUCATION + TEACH-BACK:
Acute pancreatitis + recovery + recurrence prevention + chronic complications + lifestyle
ERCP + sphincterotomy + recovery + post-ERCP precautions
Interval cholecystectomy timing + procedure + recovery + recurrence prevention
Early enteral nutrition + diet advancement + low-fat + Mediterranean-style + weight management
Pain management + opioid tapering + non-pharmacologic + chronic pain considerations
Diabetes management + insulin sliding scale + hyperglycemia + post-pancreatitis diabetes risk
Alcohol moderation/abstinence (recurrent pancreatitis prevention)
Lifestyle — weight management + exercise + Mediterranean diet + smoking cessation if applicable
Chronic disease management + DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity + family + community
Crisis resources if pain returns + new symptoms + complications
Teach-back verification of understanding + cultural sensitivity + family integration + Spanish translation

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this severe acute pancreatitis presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: SEVERE ACUTE PANCREATITIS confirmed per Atlanta 2012 (pain + lipase >3× ULN at 1200 + CT findings); GALLSTONE etiology (gallstones in gallbladder + CBD dilated 12 mm + impacted stone at ampulla of Vater + cholestatic LFT pattern); RANSON 4 at admission (moderate-severe predicted ~15% mortality); EARLY ORGAN DYSFUNCTION (mild AKI Cr 1.3 + early ARDS pattern PaO2 76 on 2L NC + lactate 3.8) → progressing to SEVERE per Atlanta
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnosis + severity classification. Foundation for management + multidisciplinary engagement. RN role: recognition + escalation + ICU activation.

opts.text: AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION with LACTATED RINGER'S (NOT normal saline — LR less hyperchloremic acidosis + may reduce SIRS) at 5-10 mL/kg/hr × 24-48 hr (500 mL/hr initial for 96 kg patient); REASSESS q2-4h based on UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr + BUN downtrending + hemodynamic improvement; AVOID excessive overload (ARDS + abdominal compartment syndrome risk)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid resuscitation framework. Cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management. RN role: fluid administration + monitoring + reassessment + escalation.

opts.text: PAIN MANAGEMENT with OPIOID PCA (hydromorphone preferred + morphine + fentanyl acceptable; meperidine no longer preferred + has toxic metabolite); SCHEDULED ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg IV q6h + ondansetron PRN; AVOID NSAIDs (renal injury + GI bleed + may worsen pancreatic inflammation + bleeding risk for ERCP); non-pharmacologic — positioning + warm compress + family + chaplain
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pain management framework. Multimodal + opioid-based + AVOID NSAIDs. RN role: medication administration + monitoring + escalation + family education.

opts.text: ELECTROLYTE replacement + monitoring — HYPOCALCEMIA common in severe pancreatitis (saponification with fatty acids; calcium gluconate 1-2 g IV slow with cardiac monitoring); HYPOMAGNESEMIA + HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA common (replace); GLUCOSE management (hyperglycemia from inflammation + DM; insulin sliding scale; target 140-180); Q4-6H BMP + LFT + lipase + calcium + Mg + Phos + lactate × first 24 hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — electrolyte + glucose framework. Common derangements + replacement essential. RN role: monitoring + medication administration + escalation.

opts.text: ERCP within 24-72 hr for gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction (URGENT 24 hr if cholangitis); sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stent if needed; PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after procedure (NSAID protective for post-ERCP pancreatitis 5-10% incidence); post-ERCP monitoring × 48 hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — ERCP for biliary obstruction. Time-sensitive. RN role: pre/post-procedure care + monitoring + family education.

opts.text: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION via NJ tube (past ligament of Treitz; or NG comparable per recent evidence) within 24-72 hr post-admission (reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity vs prolonged NPO + TPN); semi-elemental formula (low-residue + low-fat + bypass pancreatic stimulation) at 25 mL/hr advancing per protocol; transition to oral diet as tolerating
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early enteral nutrition framework. Standard current practice. RN role: NJ placement + administration + advancement + family education.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY engagement: gastroenterology + general surgery + critical care + pain medicine + nutrition + interventional radiology + infectious disease + chaplain + social worker + cultural sensitivity (Spanish interpreter + Catholic family); interval cholecystectomy 4-6 weeks; outpatient management of DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity + lifestyle + recurrence prevention
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary care framework. Standard practice. RN role: coordination + education + advocacy + family + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: COMPLICATIONS surveillance — LOCAL (peripancreatic fluid + pseudocyst + walled-off necrosis + INFECTED NECROSIS + hemorrhage + thrombosis + ascites) + SYSTEMIC (ARDS + AKI + SHOCK + sepsis + DIC + hypocalcemia + hyperglycemia + ileus + abdominal compartment syndrome); MODS in severe; antibiotics NOT routine but reserved for infected necrosis or extra-pancreatic infections (cholangitis + pneumonia)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — complications surveillance + management framework. Multi-system + multidisciplinary. RN role: monitoring + escalation + multidisciplinary + family education.

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa (rash 10 yr ago — document); NKDA other medications
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. Sulfa rash documented. No medication contraindications in current regimen. Not the priority cue but important for safety.

opts.text: Administer IBUPROFEN 800 mg PO q6h for pain control + reducing inflammation in this patient with severe acute pancreatitis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach + DANGEROUS in acute pancreatitis. NSAIDs (ibuprofen + naproxen + ketorolac + indomethacin) CONTRAINDICATED in acute pancreatitis: (1) renal injury risk in volume-depleted patient + AKI common in pancreatitis; (2) GI BLEEDING + ulcers; (3) may worsen pancreatic inflammation; (4) bleeding risk for ERCP + cholecystectomy. STANDARD = OPIOIDS first-line + acetaminophen + AVOID NSAIDs in acute pancreatitis.

opts.text: Start TPN (total parenteral nutrition) immediately + continue NPO indefinitely to 'rest the pancreas' — historical approach + reduces stimulation + improves recovery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach + outdated. EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION (within 24-72 hr) is current standard of care — reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity vs prolonged NPO + TPN. TPN reserved for refractory ileus + obstruction + severe abdominal compartment syndrome. Standard: NPO Day 1 + early enteral nutrition via NJ tube (past ligament of Treitz) + transition to oral diet as tolerating.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: ACUTE PANCREATITIS DIAGNOSIS per ATLANTA 2012 requires 2 of 3 criteria: (1) abdominal pain consistent (epigastric + radiating to back + worse supine + better leaning forward + N/V); (2) LIPASE or AMYLASE >3× upper limit normal (lipase more specific + sensitive than amylase); (3) IMAGING findings of acute pancreatitis (CT abdomen with IV contrast); SEVERITY per Atlanta = MILD (no organ failure) + MODERATELY SEVERE (transient organ failure <48 hr or local/systemic complications) + SEVERE (PERSISTENT organ failure >48 hr in 1+ systems using modified Marshall scoring)
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic + severity framework per Atlanta 2012. Standard knowledge. Foundation for diagnosis + classification + management.

opts.text: ETIOLOGIES of acute pancreatitis = GALLSTONES 40% (most common) + ALCOHOL 30% + idiopathic + post-ERCP + hypertriglyceridemia >1000 + hypercalcemia + drugs (azathioprine + valproic acid + sulfa + tetracycline + estrogen + ACE inhibitors + statins + others) + autoimmune + trauma + infections (viral mumps + viral hepatitis + CMV + EBV + bacterial) + neoplasms; this patient = GALLSTONE pancreatitis with biliary obstruction
opts.rat: CORRECT — etiology framework. Foundation for management + recurrence prevention.

opts.text: RANSON CRITERIA for severity prediction: AT ADMISSION = age >55 + WBC >16,000 + glucose >200 + AST >250 + LDH >350; AT 48 HR = HCT drop >10 + BUN rise >5 + calcium <8 + PaO2 <60 + base deficit >4 + fluid sequestration >6L; SCORES: 0-2 mild (<1% mortality); 3-4 MODERATE (15% — this patient); 5-6 severe (40%); 7+ very severe (~100%); APACHE II + BISAP also commonly used
opts.rat: CORRECT — Ranson + severity scoring framework. Standard. Foundation for prognosis + intensity of care.

opts.text: AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION with LACTATED RINGER'S (LR) preferred over NS (LR less hyperchloremic acidosis + may reduce SIRS) at 5-10 mL/kg/hr × 24-48 hr; REASSESS q2-4h based on UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr + BUN downtrending + hemodynamic improvement; AVOID excessive overload (ARDS + abdominal compartment syndrome); WATERFALL trial 2022 suggests MODERATE aggressive may be as effective with less overload risk — individualized
opts.rat: CORRECT — fluid resuscitation framework. Standard practice. Foundation for resuscitation + organ failure prevention.

opts.text: PAIN MANAGEMENT in acute pancreatitis = OPIOIDS first-line (hydromorphone + morphine + fentanyl acceptable; meperidine no longer preferred + has toxic metabolite normeperidine); SCHEDULED ACETAMINOPHEN + PCA + multimodal; AVOID NSAIDs (renal injury + GI bleeding + may worsen pancreatic inflammation + bleeding risk for ERCP/surgery); historical concern about morphine + sphincter of Oddi spasm NOT supported by current evidence
opts.rat: CORRECT — pain management framework. Standard practice. AVOID NSAIDs.

opts.text: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION (within 24-72 hr) is preferred over PROLONGED NPO + TPN — reduces infections + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity + reduces bacterial translocation + line infections + cholestasis + hyperglycemia; NJ vs NG tube comparable per recent evidence; semi-elemental formula low-residue + low-fat + pre-digested protein + medium-chain triglycerides; TPN reserved for refractory ileus + obstruction + severe abdominal compartment syndrome
opts.rat: CORRECT — nutrition framework. Standard current practice (paradigm shift from historical NPO + TPN). Foundation for outcomes.

opts.text: ERCP for GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS with cholangitis or biliary obstruction = within 24 hr URGENT if cholangitis (Charcot's triad fever + jaundice + RUQ pain; Reynolds' pentad adds shock + altered mental status — emergent + life-threatening); 24-72 hr for persistent biliary obstruction; sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stent; PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after procedure (NSAID protective for post-ERCP pancreatitis 5-10% incidence — unique exception to NSAID avoidance)
opts.rat: CORRECT — ERCP framework + indomethacin paradox (NSAID protective post-ERCP despite NSAID avoidance in pancreatitis generally). Standard practice.

opts.text: CHOLECYSTECTOMY for gallstone pancreatitis = SAME-ADMISSION (during initial hospitalization after pancreatitis improving + tolerating PO + within 1-2 weeks) for MILD CASES; INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY at 4-6 weeks after recovery for SEVERE CASES (allow complete resolution + complications) — prevents recurrence (without cholecystectomy = 20-50% recurrence rate within months); maintain ERCP + sphincterotomy in interim for biliary protection
opts.rat: CORRECT — cholecystectomy timing framework. Standard practice. Foundation for recurrence prevention.

opts.text: COMPLICATIONS surveillance + management — LOCAL (peripancreatic fluid + pseudocyst + walled-off necrosis + infected necrosis + hemorrhage + thrombosis + ascites); SYSTEMIC (ARDS + AKI + shock + sepsis + DIC + hypocalcemia + hyperglycemia + ileus + abdominal compartment); ANTIBIOTICS NOT routine but reserved for infected necrosis + extra-pancreatic infections; MULTIDISCIPLINARY = MICU + GI + surgery + IR + nutrition + pain medicine + multidisciplinary rounds
opts.rat: CORRECT — complications + multidisciplinary framework. Standard practice. Foundation for ongoing care.

opts.text: Administer IBUPROFEN 800 mg PO q6h for pain + inflammation in this patient with acute pancreatitis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. NSAIDs CONTRAINDICATED in acute pancreatitis (renal + GI + may worsen inflammation + bleeding risk for ERCP). STANDARD = opioids + acetaminophen. NOTE: PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately AFTER ERCP is the EXCEPTION (protective for post-ERCP pancreatitis 5-10% incidence) but otherwise NSAIDs avoided in pancreatitis.

opts.text: Start TPN immediately + continue NPO indefinitely to 'rest the pancreas'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach + outdated. EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION (within 24-72 hr) is current standard. STANDARD = NPO Day 1 + early enteral nutrition via NJ tube + transition to oral diet.

opts.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin) PROPHYLACTICALLY to all patients with acute pancreatitis to prevent infected necrosis + improve outcomes
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. ANTIBIOTICS NOT ROUTINELY indicated for acute pancreatitis — does NOT improve outcomes + selects for resistance + increases adverse effects. RESERVED FOR infected necrosis (clinical deterioration + organ failure + persistent fever + gas in pancreas on CT + positive cultures) or extra-pancreatic infections (cholangitis + pneumonia). Pre-ERCP antibiotics if cholangitis suspected. Standard antibiotic stewardship.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this severe acute pancreatitis presentation
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Aggressive Early Fluid Resuscitation + Organ Failure Prevention r/t severe acute pancreatitis with early organ dysfunction (mild AKI + early ARDS pattern + lactate 3.8); LR 5-10 mL/kg/hr × 24-48 hr (500 mL/hr initial for 96 kg) + reassess q2-4h based on UOP + BUN + hemodynamic; AVOID excessive overload (ARDS + abdominal compartment); continuous monitoring + arterial line + central line
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Fluid resuscitation is cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management + prevents organ failure progression + reduces complications. Time-sensitive. Foundation for all subsequent care.

opts.text: Pain Management + Multimodal r/t severe pancreatitis pain 9/10 epigastric radiating to back; HYDROMORPHONE PCA + scheduled acetaminophen + anti-emetic + non-pharmacologic; AVOID NSAIDs (renal injury + GI bleed + bleeding risk for ERCP + may worsen pancreatic inflammation); avoid meperidine (toxic metabolite normeperidine)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Severe pain is hallmark + requires aggressive multimodal management. Foundation for patient comfort + recovery + cooperation. AVOID NSAIDs critical.

opts.text: ERCP + Biliary Obstruction Treatment r/t gallstone pancreatitis with CBD dilated 12 mm + impacted stone at ampulla of Vater; ERCP scheduled Day 2 within 24-72 hr (URGENT 24 hr if cholangitis); sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stent; PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after for post-ERCP pancreatitis prevention; post-ERCP monitoring × 48 hr; INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY 4-6 weeks
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. ERCP addresses underlying biliary obstruction + reduces complications + standard for gallstone pancreatitis. Interval cholecystectomy prevents recurrence.

opts.text: Early Enteral Nutrition + Electrolyte Replacement r/t severe pancreatitis nutrition management; EARLY ENTERAL via NJ tube (past ligament of Treitz) within 24-72 hr post-admission (Day 3 after ERCP for this patient); semi-elemental formula low-residue + low-fat + advancing per protocol; transition to oral diet as tolerating; ELECTROLYTE replacement = HYPOCALCEMIA (saponification — calcium gluconate IV slow with cardiac monitoring) + magnesium + phosphate; insulin sliding scale for hyperglycemia
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + ongoing. Early enteral nutrition reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity vs prolonged NPO + TPN. Electrolyte replacement essential.

opts.text: Complications Surveillance + Multidisciplinary Engagement r/t severe pancreatitis complications (local: peripancreatic fluid + pseudocyst + walled-off necrosis + infected necrosis + hemorrhage + thrombosis + ascites; systemic: ARDS + AKI + shock + sepsis + DIC + electrolyte + ileus + abdominal compartment); MULTIDISCIPLINARY = MICU + GI + surgery + IR + nutrition + pain medicine + chaplain + social worker + family + culturally sensitive (Spanish interpreter + Catholic family)
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + ongoing. Comprehensive surveillance + multidisciplinary engagement + family-centered care. Foundation for ongoing care + recovery.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this severe acute pancreatitis.

opts.text: AGGRESSIVE EARLY FLUID RESUSCITATION with LACTATED RINGER'S (NOT NS) at 5-10 mL/kg/hr × 24-48 hr (500 mL/hr initial for 96 kg); REASSESS q2-4h based on UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr + BUN downtrending + hemodynamic improvement; AVOID excessive overload (ARDS + abdominal compartment); transition to maintenance 150 mL/hr when responsive
opts.rat: Standard cornerstone of acute pancreatitis management. Prevents organ failure + reduces complications. Standard practice.

opts.text: PAIN MANAGEMENT with HYDROMORPHONE PCA + scheduled ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg IV q6h + ONDANSETRON PRN nausea + non-pharmacologic (positioning + warm compress + family + chaplain); AVOID NSAIDs (renal + GI + bleeding risk for ERCP + may worsen pancreatic inflammation); transition to PO oxycodone when tolerating
opts.rat: Standard pain management. Opioid + acetaminophen + multimodal. AVOID NSAIDs. Standard practice.

opts.text: ELECTROLYTE replacement — CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1-2 g IV slowly with cardiac monitoring (hypocalcemia common from saponification) + MAGNESIUM 2-4 g IV + PHOSPHATE replacement + POTASSIUM as needed; INSULIN sliding scale for hyperglycemia (target 140-180); Q4-6H BMP + LFT + lipase + calcium + Mg + Phos + lactate
opts.rat: Standard electrolyte + glucose management in severe pancreatitis. Hypocalcemia common + critical. Standard practice.

opts.text: ERCP within 24-72 hr for gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction; sphincterotomy + stone extraction + biliary stent; PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after procedure (NSAID protective for post-ERCP pancreatitis); post-ERCP monitoring × 48 hr + serial lipase + recurrent pain assessment; INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY 4-6 weeks after recovery
opts.rat: Standard ERCP for gallstone pancreatitis with biliary obstruction. Prophylactic indomethacin paradox (exception to NSAID avoidance). Interval cholecystectomy recurrence prevention. Standard practice.

opts.text: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION via NJ tube (past ligament of Treitz; or NG comparable per recent evidence) starting Day 3 (within 24-72 hr post-admission); semi-elemental formula (low-residue + low-fat + bypass pancreatic stimulation) at 25 mL/hr advancing per protocol; transition to oral diet as tolerating + pain controlled + LFTs improving
opts.rat: Standard early enteral nutrition. Reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity. Current evidence-based practice.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY engagement: gastroenterology + general surgery + critical care + pain medicine + nutrition + interventional radiology + infectious disease + chaplain + social worker + cultural sensitivity (Spanish interpreter + Catholic family); interval cholecystectomy 4-6 weeks; outpatient management of DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity + lifestyle
opts.rat: Standard multidisciplinary care + family-centered + culturally sensitive. Foundation for ongoing care + outcomes.

opts.text: Administer IBUPROFEN 800 mg PO q6h for pain + reducing inflammation in this patient with severe acute pancreatitis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. NSAIDs CONTRAINDICATED in acute pancreatitis (renal injury + GI bleeding + may worsen pancreatic inflammation + bleeding risk for ERCP). EXCEPTION = prophylactic rectal indomethacin AFTER ERCP for post-ERCP pancreatitis prevention. Standard = opioids + acetaminophen + AVOID NSAIDs generally.

opts.text: Start TPN immediately + continue NPO indefinitely to 'rest the pancreas' for at least 5-7 days before considering any enteral nutrition
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach + outdated. EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION (within 24-72 hr) is current standard. Reduces infection + LOS + mortality + supports gut integrity vs prolonged NPO + TPN. Standard: NPO Day 1 + early enteral via NJ tube + transition to oral.

opts.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin) PROPHYLACTICALLY to all patients with acute pancreatitis to prevent infected necrosis + improve outcomes
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. Antibiotics NOT routinely indicated for acute pancreatitis — does NOT improve outcomes + selects for resistance + increases adverse effects. RESERVED for infected necrosis (clinical deterioration + organ failure + persistent fever + gas in pancreas + positive cultures) or extra-pancreatic infections (cholangitis + pneumonia). Pre-ERCP antibiotics if cholangitis suspected. Standard antibiotic stewardship.

opts.text: Administer MEPERIDINE (Demerol) 50 mg IV q3-4h for pain in this patient because it does not cause sphincter of Oddi spasm like morphine
opts.rat: ⚠️ NOT preferred. MEPERIDINE was historically preferred for sphincter of Oddi concern but is NO LONGER FIRST-LINE — has TOXIC METABOLITE NORMEPERIDINE accumulating with renal impairment + elderly + chronic use → seizures + neurotoxicity. Current evidence shows MORPHINE + HYDROMORPHONE acceptable without significant sphincter of Oddi worsening. Standard: HYDROMORPHONE or MORPHINE PCA preferred.

opts.text: PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg immediately after ERCP procedure to prevent post-ERCP pancreatitis (5-10% incidence)
opts.rat: EXCEPTION to NSAID avoidance — RECTAL INDOMETHACIN IS PROTECTIVE for post-ERCP pancreatitis (NSAID anti-inflammatory effect + COX inhibition + reduces post-ERCP pancreatitis incidence per multiple RCTs). Standard practice post-ERCP. RN role: medication administration + monitoring + family education.

opts.text: INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY at 4-6 weeks after recovery from severe pancreatitis (vs same-admission for mild cases) to prevent recurrence (without cholecystectomy = 20-50% recurrence rate within months)
opts.rat: Standard cholecystectomy planning for gallstone pancreatitis. Interval for severe + same-admission for mild. Prevents recurrence. Standard practice.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at severe acute pancreatitis admission
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: ESTABLISH 2 large-bore IVs (16-gauge) + arterial line + central line + Foley + continuous cardiac monitoring + pulse oximetry + STAT labs (CBC + BMP + LFT + lipase + amylase + lactate + ABG + coags + ammonia + triglycerides + calcium + magnesium + phosphate + CRP + procalcitonin + blood cultures + UA); IV LACTATED RINGER'S (NOT NS) at 500 mL/hr initial (5-10 mL/kg/hr); CT abdomen with IV contrast ordered
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. IV access + STAT labs + aggressive LR resuscitation + imaging. Foundation for diagnosis + management. RN role: rapid + coordinated.

opts.text: PAIN MANAGEMENT + ANTI-EMETIC: HYDROMORPHONE 1 mg IV bolus + PCA pump 0.2 mg/dose with 10 min lock-out max 1.2 mg/hr; SCHEDULED ACETAMINOPHEN 1000 mg IV q6h; ONDANSETRON 4 mg IV q6h PRN nausea; AVOID NSAIDs + meperidine; non-pharmacologic positioning + warm compress
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Severe pain management essential + multimodal. AVOID NSAIDs + meperidine. RN role: medication administration + monitoring.

opts.text: MICU TRANSFER + MULTIDISCIPLINARY ACTIVATION: GI + general surgery + critical care + pain medicine + nutrition + interventional radiology + infectious disease + chaplain + social worker; ERCP scheduled within 24-72 hr (URGENT 24 hr if cholangitis); CONTINUOUS MONITORING + arterial line + central line + Foley + I/O hourly + daily weights + serial labs + complications surveillance
opts.rat: #3 — Third. MICU + multidisciplinary engagement. ERCP planning. Standard practice. RN role: coordination + advocacy + escalation.

opts.text: ELECTROLYTE REPLACEMENT + GLUCOSE MANAGEMENT: CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1-2 g IV slow with cardiac monitoring (hypocalcemia common from saponification) + MAGNESIUM 2-4 g IV + PHOSPHATE + POTASSIUM as needed; INSULIN sliding scale for hyperglycemia (target 140-180); Q4-6H labs + complications surveillance
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Electrolyte + glucose management essential. Standard practice. RN role: medication administration + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION + DISCHARGE PLANNING: NJ TUBE PLACEMENT (past ligament of Treitz) at end of Day 2 ERCP for early enteral nutrition starting Day 3; semi-elemental formula at 25 mL/hr advancing per protocol; transition to oral diet as tolerating; INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY 4-6 weeks; multidisciplinary outpatient + DM + HTN + hyperlipidemia + obesity + lifestyle + family + cultural sensitivity
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing + foundational. Early enteral nutrition + discharge planning + multidisciplinary outpatient. Foundation for sustained recovery.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: MICU admission 1200: HR 98 NSR + BP 122/76 MAP 91 + RR 20 + SpO2 97% on 2L NC + T 37.6 + pain 5/10 (improved from 9/10) + arterial line + central line + Foley + LR 250 mL/hr + UOP 30 mL/hr (initially low; needs to improve); calcium 7.6 replaced; lactate 3.8 (improving); multidisciplinary engagement; family at bedside
opts.rat: Standard expected initial response to aggressive resuscitation + pain management. Foundation for ongoing care. RN role: continued monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: MICU 2000 (8 hr post admission): HR 92 + BP 128/78 + RR 18 + SpO2 97% + T 37.4 + pain 4/10 + UOP 60 mL/hr (improved); total IVF 12 hr ~6.5 L; BUN 24 (downtrending — fluid responsive); Cr 1.1 (improving); calcium 8.4 + Mg 1.9; ERCP scheduled tomorrow; NJ tube placement at end of ERCP
opts.rat: Standard expected response to aggressive LR + supportive care. Foundation for ongoing recovery + ERCP planning.

opts.text: Day 2 morning (pre-ERCP): HR 84 + BP 128/76 + RR 16 + SpO2 98% RA (weaning from 2L) + T 37.2 + pain 3/10 + tolerating sips clear water; UOP 80 mL/hr; total IVF 24 hr ~10 L; RANSON 48 HR = 4 (cumulative; 15% mortality); LIPASE 850 (downtrending) + bilirubin 6.2 (peaked) + WBC 16,500 + lactate 2.0; calcium 8.4 + Mg 2.0; ERCP 0900
opts.rat: Standard expected Day 2 trajectory + DKA resolving + transition to ERCP + supportive care. Foundation for treatment of underlying biliary obstruction.

opts.text: Day 2 ERCP successful: sphincterotomy + stone extraction (1.5 cm stone removed) + biliary stent; PROPHYLACTIC RECTAL INDOMETHACIN 100 mg given immediately after for post-ERCP pancreatitis prevention; NJ tube placed at end of procedure; awake + alert post-procedure + back to MICU + monitoring × 48 hr
opts.rat: Successful ERCP + biliary obstruction treated + indomethacin for prevention + NJ tube + monitoring. Foundation for ongoing recovery + interval cholecystectomy.

opts.text: Day 3 morning (post-ERCP + early enteral nutrition): HR 82 + BP 130/78 + RR 16 + SpO2 98% RA + T 37.0 + pain 2/10 (transitioning to PO oxycodone) + ERCP-related no new symptoms + EARLY ENTERAL NUTRITION started via NJ at 25 mL/hr; bilirubin downtrending 4.6 + lipase 420 + AST 140 + ALT 100; WBC 14,000 + CRP 120 + calcium 8.8; UOP 100 mL/hr; INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY 4-6 weeks planned
opts.rat: Standard expected Day 3 recovery + ERCP + early enteral nutrition + interval cholecystectomy planning. Foundation for ongoing recovery + outpatient transition.

opts.text: Day 5 ALTERNATIVE — patient developed acute respiratory failure + bilateral pulmonary edema on CXR = ARDS from severe pancreatitis; intubation + mechanical ventilation with lung-protective Vt 6 mL/kg IBW + PEEP 10 + FiO2 60%; sedation + analgesia; continued aggressive fluid resuscitation tempered by ARDS; multidisciplinary critical care + pulmonary consultation + family meeting
opts.rat: ARDS = severe pancreatitis complication. Aggressive supportive care + mechanical ventilation + lung-protective + multidisciplinary critical care. RN role: recognition + escalation + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: Day 7 ALTERNATIVE — patient developed acute deterioration with persistent fever + WBC 22,000 + worsening abdominal pain + hypotension; CT abdomen at 72 hr post-onset reveals INFECTED PANCREATIC NECROSIS with gas in pancreas; cultures pending + broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin); IR percutaneous drainage + endoscopic step-up approach + multidisciplinary + ID + general surgery + IR
opts.rat: INFECTED PANCREATIC NECROSIS = severe complication of severe pancreatitis. Standard response = antibiotics + percutaneous drainage + step-up approach + multidisciplinary. RN role: monitoring + escalation + multidisciplinary.

opts.text: Day 10 — patient discharged to floor + tolerating oral diet (low-fat + Mediterranean-style) + pain controlled with PO oxycodone PRN + transitioning to acetaminophen + multidisciplinary outpatient appointments scheduled + GI follow-up within 2 weeks + general surgery for INTERVAL CHOLECYSTECTOMY at 4-6 weeks + PCP medical home for chronic disease management + diabetes + lifestyle + family supportive + chaplain ongoing + Spanish interpreter for follow-up
opts.rat: Standard expected Day 10 discharge + comprehensive outpatient transition + interval cholecystectomy planning. Foundation for sustained recovery + long-term management.

opts.text: Week 6 outpatient — INTERVAL LAPAROSCOPIC CHOLECYSTECTOMY completed successfully + recovery uneventful + outpatient follow-up + lifestyle modifications + alcohol moderation + Mediterranean-style diet + weight management + diabetes management + insurance + community resources + family supportive + grateful for care
opts.rat: Standard expected interval cholecystectomy + recurrence prevention + comprehensive outpatient care + lifestyle modifications. Foundation for long-term recovery + prevention.

opts.text: 1-year follow-up: SUSTAINED RECOVERY × 1 year + no recurrence after cholecystectomy + lifestyle modifications maintained + Mediterranean diet + weight loss 8 kg + DM well-controlled HbA1c 6.8 + cardiovascular optimization + family supportive + community + cultural + spiritual continued + grateful for comprehensive care; advocacy for early recognition + treatment of pancreatitis + recurrence prevention
opts.rat: Excellent 1-year outcome + sustained recovery + recurrence prevention + lifestyle modifications + chronic disease management + family + community + cultural sensitivity. Standard ideal long-term trajectory.
